name: whylabs-toolkit
documentation:
# WhyLabs Toolkit

The WhyLabs Toolkit package contains helper methods to help users interact with our internal APIs. Users will benefit from using it if they want to abstract some of WhyLabs' internal logic and also automate recurring API calls.


## Basic usage
To start using the `whylabs_toolkit` package, install it from PyPI with:
```bash
pip install whylabs_toolkit
``` 

## Packages

The available packages that we have enable different use-cases for the `whylabs_toolkit`. To get started, navigate to one of the following sections and find useful tutorials there.

| Package                                                                                                                   | Usage                                                                  |
|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| [Monitor Manager](https://github.com/whylabs/whylabs-toolkit/blob/mainline/whylabs_toolkit/monitor/manager/README.md)     | Author and modify existing WhyLabs monitor with Python.                |
| [Monitor Diagnoser](https://github.com/whylabs/whylabs-toolkit/blob/mainline/whylabs_toolkit/monitor/diagnoser/README.md) | Diagnose problems with monitors.                                       |
| [WhyLabs Helpers](https://github.com/whylabs/whylabs-toolkit/blob/mainline/whylabs_toolkit/helpers/README.md)             | Interact with and modify your Datasets and ML Models specs in WhyLabs. |

## Development

To start contributing, you will manage dependencies with [Poetry](https://python-poetry.org/) and also a handful of `Makefile` commands. To install all necessary dependencies and activate the virtual environment, run:

```bash
make setup && poetry shell
```

## Get in touch
If you want to learn more how you can benefit from this package or if there is anything missing, please [contact our support](https://whylabs.ai/contact-us), we'll be more than happy to help you!

modules.name: whylabs_toolkit.monitor.models.utils
documentation: Common utilities.

functions.name: whylabs_toolkit.monitor.models.utils.anyOf_to_oneOf
documentation:
Turn anyOf in JSON schema to oneOf.

onfOf is much stricter and pyDantic doesn't produce this tag. We hijack the JSON schema object to
set this correctly.

See: https://github.com/samuelcolvin/pydantic/issues/656

classes.name: whylabs_toolkit.monitor.models.column_schema.WeightConfig

class_variables.name: whylabs_toolkit.monitor.models.column_schema.WeightConfig.weights
documentation: type: ignore

Object that specifies column weights.

- By default, the weight of a column is None (unspecified)
- If the weight is unspecified, the column is EXCLUDED when you perform a filter/sort by weight
- For sorting, unweighted column take the LEAST PRECEDENCE, meaning that weight column have higher priorities
- They are not hierarchical: if a segment weight config is specified and a column does not have a weight in that
config, we will not use any hierarchy to resolve the value. It will be None
- Order of unweighted column is undefined.

modules.name: whylabs_toolkit.monitor.models.analyzer.baseline
documentation: Define various baselines.

documentation: Supported baseline types.

documentation: Base class for a baseline.

classes.name: whylabs_toolkit.monitor.models.analyzer.baseline.ReferenceProfileId
documentation:
A baseline based on a static reference profile.

A typical use case is to use a "gold" dataset and upload its profile to WhyLabs. This can be a training dataset
as well for an ML model.

classes.name: whylabs_toolkit.monitor.models.analyzer.baseline.TrailingWindowBaseline
documentation:
A dynamic trailing window.

This is useful if you don't have a static baseline to monitor against. This is the default mode for most
monitors.

classes.name: whylabs_toolkit.monitor.models.analyzer.baseline.TimeRangeBaseline
documentation:
A static time range.

Instead of using a single profile or a trailing window, user can lock in a "good" period.

classes.name: whylabs_toolkit.monitor.models.analyzer.baseline.SingleBatchBaseline
documentation:
Using current batch.

This is used when you want to use one batch to monitor another batch in a different metric entity.

noinspection PyUnusedLocal
Updates JSON schema anyOf to oneOf.

Configuration for running an analysis.

An analysis targets a metric (note that a metric could be a complex object) for one or multiple fields in
one or multiple segments. The output is a list of 'anomalies' that might show issues with data.

classes.name: whylabs_toolkit.monitor.models.analyzer.algorithms.ComparisonConfig
documentation:
Compare whether the target against a value or against a baseline's metric.

This is useful to detect data type change, for instance.

classes.name: whylabs_toolkit.monitor.models.analyzer.algorithms.ColumnListChangeConfig
documentation:
Compare whether the target is equal to a value or not.

This is useful to detect data type change, for instance.

classes.name: whylabs_toolkit.monitor.models.analyzer.algorithms.FixedThresholdsConfig
documentation:
Fixed threshold analysis.

If user fails to set both upper bound and lower bound, this algorithm becomes a no-op.
WhyLabs might enforce the present of either fields in the future.

classes.name: whylabs_toolkit.monitor.models.analyzer.algorithms.ThresholdType

class_variables.name: whylabs_toolkit.monitor.models.analyzer.algorithms.ThresholdType.lower
documentation: type: ignore

class_variables.name: whylabs_toolkit.monitor.models.analyzer.algorithms.ThresholdType.upper
documentation: type: ignore

By default, an anomaly will be generated when the target is above or below the baseline
by the specified threshold.

If its only desirable to alert when the target is above the
baseline and not the other way around, specify upper for your ThresholdType.

classes.name: whylabs_toolkit.monitor.models.analyzer.algorithms.StddevConfig
documentation:
Calculates upper bounds and lower bounds based on stddev from a series of numbers.

An analyzer using stddev for a window of time range.

This calculation will fall back to Poisson distribution if there is only 1 value in the baseline.
For 2 values, we use the formula sqrt((x_i - avg(x))^2 / n - 1)

classes.name: whylabs_toolkit.monitor.models.analyzer.algorithms.SeasonalConfig
documentation:
An analyzer using stddev for a window of time range.

This will fall back to Poisson distribution if there is only 1 value in the baseline.

This only works with TrailingWindow baseline (TODO: add backend validation)

classes.name: whylabs_toolkit.monitor.models.analyzer.algorithms.DriftConfig
documentation:
An analyzer using stddev for a window of time range.

This analysis will detect whether the data drifts or not. By default, we use hellinger distance with a threshold
of 0.7.

classes.name: whylabs_toolkit.helpers.schema.UpdateEntityDataTypes
documentation:
Update data types on each column of the dataset

Arguments
----
columns_schema: Dict[str, ColumnDataType]
    The keys are column names and the values are the
    desired data_types, as the example below shows

```python
columns_schema = {
    "column_1": ColumnDataType.fractional,
    "column_2": ColumnDataType.boolean,
    "column_3": ColumnDataType.string,
}
```

These are the currently supported data types:
---
- integral
- fractional
- bool
- string
- unknown
- null
---
